mcqs.question: What is the most severe type of virtualization attack?

options.text: VM Sprawl
VM Escape
Snapshot theft
Live migration interception

mcqs.explanation: VM Escape breaks the fundamental isolation between VMs by exploiting hypervisor vulnerabilities, allowing an attacker to access the host and all other VMs.

mcqs.question: Which hardware technology provides CPU-level isolation for VMs?

options.text: Intel VT-d
Intel VT-x / AMD-V
SR-IOV
TPM

mcqs.explanation: Intel VT-x and AMD-V provide hardware-assisted CPU virtualization with separate execution contexts. VT-d handles I/O isolation, SR-IOV handles network device sharing.

mcqs.question: Why should secrets NEVER be embedded in VM golden images?

options.text: It makes the image file too large
Every VM from that image shares the same credentials
Secrets cannot be stored in image formats
It slows down VM boot time

mcqs.explanation: Embedding secrets means every VM deployed from that image has identical credentials. If one is compromised, all VMs from that image are compromised.

mcqs.question: What is the primary security risk of live VM migration?

options.text: The VM loses its IP address
The destination host may be less powerful
VM memory state containing secrets can be intercepted
The VM must be rebooted after migration

mcqs.explanation: During live migration, the entire VM state including memory (encryption keys, session tokens, passwords) is transferred over the network and can be intercepted if unencrypted.

mcqs.question: Why is micro-segmentation necessary for VM security?

options.text: It encrypts data at rest in VMs
Traditional firewalls cannot see east-west traffic between VMs
It speeds up VM boot time
It replaces the need for a hypervisor

mcqs.explanation: Traditional perimeter firewalls are blind to traffic between VMs on the same host. Micro-segmentation uses SDN to create granular policies, limiting lateral movement after breach.
